# Post Frameworks

Detailed templates, examples, and engagement tips for each of the 7 post frameworks. Select the framework that best matches the topic, audience, and intent. When in doubt, start with Story or Listicle -- they have the broadest applicability. These frameworks are platform-agnostic at the structural level; see the Platform Variants subsection in each framework for adaptation notes.

---

## 1. Story

### When to Use

Use the Story framework when the post centers on a personal narrative that leads to a business lesson. Stories create emotional connection and are the highest-engagement format across platforms because they combine vulnerability with authority. Choose this framework when the topic can be anchored in a specific event, conversation, decision, or turning point.

### Ideal Audience Segments

- **Primary**: Founder-CEO (peers connect with founder journeys)
- **Secondary**: All segments -- stories are universal when the lesson is relevant

### Recommended Length Mode

Works at all lengths. Short stories land as punchy anecdotes. Medium stories allow a full narrative arc. Long stories support detailed timelines and multiple lessons.

### Structure Template

```
Line 1-2: HOOK -- Drop the reader into the story mid-action or at the moment of tension.
           Use a specific detail (date, dollar amount, name, place).

Line 3-5: SETUP -- Provide just enough context for the reader to understand the stakes.
           Who, what, where -- in 2-3 sentences max.

Line 6-10: CONFLICT -- Describe the challenge, mistake, or turning point.
            This is the core of the story. Make the reader feel the tension.
            Include one specific detail that makes it real and unrepeatable.

Line 11-13: RESOLUTION -- What happened as a result.
             State the outcome in concrete terms (revenue, time, relationship, decision).

Line 14-16: LESSON -- Extract the universal takeaway.
             Frame it as applicable to the reader, not just the writer.
             One sentence for the lesson. One sentence for the CTA.

Hashtags: 3-5 relevant tags on the final line.
```

### Example Post

```
Two years ago, I fired my biggest client.

They represented 40% of our revenue.

The decision took me 3 months to make and 3 minutes to execute.

Here is what pushed me over the edge: they called my developer at 11pm on a Saturday to "check on progress."

That was the third time that quarter.

I did the math. The stress, the turnover risk, the after-hours work -- that client was costing us more than they paid.

Within 6 months, we replaced that revenue with 3 smaller clients who respected boundaries.

Lesson: Your biggest client should never be your most important client.

Who have you been afraid to fire?

#Entrepreneurship #ClientManagement #FounderLife #SmallBusiness
```

### Engagement Tips

- Open in medias res -- drop the reader into the middle of the action, not at the beginning.
- Include one specific, unrepeatable detail (a date, a dollar amount, a direct quote). Specificity is what separates a story from a platitude.
- The lesson must be separable from the story. A reader who skips straight to the lesson should still get value.
- End with a question that invites the reader to share their version of the story.
- Avoid lessons that are obvious or generic ("Hard work pays off"). The best story posts deliver a lesson that surprises.

### Platform Variants

**X/Twitter**: Each thread tweet = one beat of the narrative. Tweet 1 = hook (mid-action entry). Tweet 2 = setup/stakes. Tweet 3-4 = conflict with the specific detail. Tweet 5 = resolution + lesson. Tweet 6 = CTA. Keep each tweet as a standalone scene -- readers may encounter any single tweet via retweet.

**Meta (FB)**: Emphasize emotional resonance and relatability. Facebook audiences engage most with stories that feel personal and unpolished. Lead with the feeling ("I was terrified") rather than the fact ("Revenue dropped 40%"). Include a visual if possible -- a photo from the moment, a screenshot, or a relevant image that grounds the story.

**Meta (IG)**: Caption references the accompanying visual. The image or carousel carries the emotional weight; the caption provides the narrative context. First line = the hook (under 125 chars). Consider splitting a longer story across carousel slides with the caption providing the lesson and CTA.

**TikTok**: Structure as hook (3s) + conflict (15s) + resolution (10s) + CTA (3s). The hook must be visual and verbal simultaneously -- say the most surprising part of the story while showing something attention-grabbing. Keep the narrative to one single turning point. No subplots. Caption = hook line + 3-5 hashtags.

---

## 2. Listicle

### When to Use

Use the Listicle framework when the post delivers multiple discrete insights, tips, observations, or recommendations. Listicles are inherently scannable and perform well on mobile. Choose this framework when the topic naturally breaks into 3-10 parallel items of roughly equal weight.

### Ideal Audience Segments

- **Primary**: Marketer (loves tactical lists), Technical (loves tool/technique lists)
- **Secondary**: Founder-CEO (practical tip lists)

### Recommended Length Mode

Medium to Long. Short listicles (3-4 items) work at Medium. Longer listicles (5-10 items) require Long mode. Never exceed 10 items.

### Structure Template

```
Line 1-2: HOOK -- State the number and the promise.
           "N things I learned about X after Y."
           The number and the qualifier create specificity.

Line 3: Blank line for visual separation after fold.

Line 4-N: LIST ITEMS -- One item per visual block.
           Each item: number + bold label + 1-2 sentence explanation.
           Maintain parallel structure across items (all start with verbs,
           or all start with nouns -- pick one and hold it).

           Space between each item with a blank line.
           Order by impact: strongest items first and last (primacy and recency effect).

Line N+1: CLOSER -- Summarize the through-line across all items in 1 sentence.
           Add an engagement CTA.

Hashtags: 3-5 relevant tags on the final line.
```

### Example Post

```
7 things I stopped doing that doubled my output as a founder:

1. Checking email before 10am
Mornings are for deep work, not reactive triage.

2. Saying "let me think about it" in meetings
Now I say yes or no on the spot. Faster decisions, fewer bottlenecks.

3. Writing proposals from scratch
I built 3 templates. 90% of the work is now done before I start.

4. Attending meetings without an agenda
No agenda, no meeting. This alone saved 5 hours per week.

5. Designing before validating
I stopped building features nobody asked for. Revenue went up.

6. Hiring for credentials
I hire for proof of work now. The best people show, not tell.

7. Working weekends "just to catch up"
Rest is not a reward. It is a requirement.

The pattern: every gain came from subtracting, not adding.

Which one would change your week the most?

#Productivity #FounderTips #Leadership #StartupLife #TimeManagement
```

### Engagement Tips

- Odd numbers (5, 7, 9) outperform even numbers in engagement. They feel less manufactured.
- The strongest items belong at positions 1 and last (primacy and recency effects). Bury the weakest item at position 3 or 4.
- Each item must be independently valuable. A reader who only reads item 4 should still get a complete insight.
- Add one item that is slightly surprising or counterintuitive. This drives comments from people who disagree.
- The hook must include a specific number AND a qualifier that implies personal experience ("after 5 years", "this quarter", "the hard way").
- Invite readers to add their own item in the comments. This drives engagement and extends reach.

### Platform Variants

**X/Twitter**: One list item per tweet. Tweet 1 = hook with the number and promise. Tweets 2-N = one item each (number + insight in under 280 chars). Final tweet = the through-line summary + CTA. This format is the most natural listicle-to-thread mapping. Use (1/N) counters when exceeding 3 tweets.

**Meta (FB)**: Condense to the top 3-5 items for optimal engagement. Facebook audiences prefer shorter lists with more context per item. Pair with a carousel image or infographic that visualizes the list. The post text provides the narrative framing; the visual provides the scannable list.

**Meta (IG)**: Carousel format is ideal -- one list item per slide. The caption provides the hook, the through-line, and the CTA. Each carousel slide should be visually designed with the item number, label, and a one-line explanation. Caption references "swipe through" or "save this carousel."

**TikTok**: Rapid-fire delivery. Each list item = 3-5 seconds on screen (text overlay + verbal). Hook the first 3 seconds with "N things that [outcome]" spoken directly to camera. Keep to 5 items maximum for a 30-45 second video. Caption = hook line + hashtags.

---

## 3. Contrarian Take

### When to Use

Use the Contrarian Take framework when the post challenges a widely accepted belief, practice, or trend. This framework generates the highest comment volume because it invites debate. Choose it when the topic has a "conventional wisdom" that deserves questioning and the writer has evidence or experience to support an alternative view.

### Ideal Audience Segments

- **Primary**: Founder-CEO (attracts debate from experienced operators)
- **Secondary**: Corporate-CXO (challenges at the strategic level)

### Recommended Length Mode

Short to Medium. The power of a contrarian take is in its sharpness. Long contrarian posts dilute the punch. State the position, support it briefly, and let the comments do the work.

### Structure Template

```
Line 1-2: HOOK -- State the contrarian position directly.
           "Everyone says X. I think Y."
           or "Unpopular opinion: [bold claim]."
           Make the disagreement immediate and unambiguous.

Line 3-5: CONVENTIONAL WISDOM -- Acknowledge what most people believe and why.
           Show that the writer understands the mainstream position.
           This builds credibility -- the take is informed, not ignorant.

Line 6-9: THE COUNTER -- Present the alternative view with evidence.
           Ground the take in personal experience, data, or specific examples.
           One strong piece of evidence beats three weak ones.

Line 10-12: NUANCE -- Acknowledge where the conventional wisdom does hold.
             This prevents the post from reading as absolutist.
             "This applies when X. If you are doing Y, the old advice still works."

Line 13-14: CLOSER -- Restate the position in one sentence.
             Invite the debate: "Am I wrong?"

Hashtags: 3-5 relevant tags on the final line.
```

### Example Post

```
Everyone says "hire slow, fire fast."

I think that is dangerous advice.

Here is why:

"Hire slow" has become an excuse for indecision. I have seen founders take 4 months to fill a role that costs them $50K in lost revenue every month.

The real advice should be "hire deliberately, with a deadline."

Set a 30-day hiring window. Define the role in week 1. Source in week 2. Interview in week 3. Decide in week 4.

Yes, you might make a mistake. But a bad hire you catch in 90 days costs less than a vacancy you tolerate for 120.

The exception: leadership hires. Take your time on those. Everyone else? Move.

Hire deliberately. Not slowly.

Am I wrong?

#Hiring #StartupHiring #Leadership #FounderAdvice
```

### Engagement Tips

- The hook must create disagreement in the first two lines. If a reader can nod along without friction, the take is not contrarian enough.
- Always acknowledge the conventional wisdom before dismantling it. Attacking a straw man is transparent and damages credibility.
- Include one specific number, example, or experience. Contrarian takes without evidence are just hot takes.
- End with an explicit invitation to disagree. "Am I wrong?" or "Where does this break down?" signals confidence and invites comments.
- Avoid being contrarian for its own sake. The take must be genuinely held and defensible. Readers detect performative disagreement.
- The nuance section is mandatory. Pure absolutism attracts trolls instead of thoughtful debate.

### Platform Variants

**X/Twitter**: The single-tweet format is ideal for short contrarian takes. State the position in one tweet and let quote-tweets do the debating. For medium takes, use a 2-3 tweet thread: tweet 1 = the bold claim, tweet 2 = the evidence, tweet 3 = the nuance + "Am I wrong?" X thrives on debate -- this framework gets the most retweets and quote-tweets.

**Meta (FB)**: Lead with the emotional angle of the disagreement. Facebook audiences engage with takes that feel personally experienced rather than intellectually argued. "Everyone told me to hire slow. I listened. It almost killed my company." works better than "Unpopular opinion: hire slow is bad advice." Expect long comment threads -- engage with the first 5-10 replies.

**Meta (IG)**: Works best as a text-on-image post (bold claim on a clean background) with the caption providing the nuance and evidence. First caption line = the contrarian claim. The visual hooks the scroll; the caption delivers the substance. Carousel format also works: slide 1 = the take, slides 2-3 = the evidence, final slide = "Agree or disagree? Comment below."

**TikTok**: Open directly to camera with the bold claim in the first 3 seconds. "Everyone says [X]. They are wrong." Body (15-20s) = deliver the counter with one concrete example. Close (3-5s) = "Fight me in the comments." The confrontational format drives TikTok engagement. Keep the nuance minimal -- TikTok rewards conviction over qualification.

---

## 4. How-To

### When to Use

Use the How-To framework when the post teaches a specific, repeatable process. The reader should be able to implement the technique after reading the post. Choose this framework when the topic involves a concrete method, workflow, or technique that can be broken into sequential steps.

### Ideal Audience Segments

- **Primary**: Technical (values actionable processes)
- **Secondary**: Marketer (values tactical playbooks)

### Recommended Length Mode

Medium to Long. Short how-tos rarely have enough room for meaningful steps. Medium works for 3-4 step processes. Long mode accommodates 5-7 steps with context.

### Structure Template

```
Line 1-2: HOOK -- State the outcome the reader will achieve.
           Lead with the result, not the process.
           "How to [achieve outcome] in [timeframe]" or
           "[Outcome] in [N] steps."

Line 3-4: CONTEXT -- One sentence on why this matters or who this is for.
           One sentence on what makes this approach different from the obvious one.

Line 5-N: STEPS -- Numbered steps, one per visual block.
           Each step: number + action verb label + 1-2 sentence explanation.
           Include specific tools, settings, values, or inputs.
           Each step must produce a verifiable intermediate result.

           Order steps sequentially -- the output of step N feeds step N+1.

Line N+1-N+2: RESULT -- State what the reader now has.
               Quantify the outcome if possible (time saved, output produced).

Line N+3: CTA -- Invite the reader to share their version or ask questions.

Hashtags: 3-5 relevant tags on the final line.
```

### Example Post

```
How to write a cold email that gets replies (in under 5 minutes):

Most cold emails fail because they talk about the sender. Flip it.

Step 1: Research for 60 seconds
Find one specific thing about the prospect -- a recent post, a company announcement, a product launch. Use it in line 1.

Step 2: Open with their win, not your pitch
"Saw you just launched [product]. The onboarding flow is clever."

Step 3: State the problem you solve in one sentence
No features. No jargon. Just the outcome. "I help [type of company] cut [metric] by [amount]."

Step 4: Make the ask tiny
"Worth a 15-minute call this week?" Not "I'd love to schedule a comprehensive demo of our platform."

Step 5: Keep it under 80 words total
If your cold email scrolls on mobile, it will not get read.

Result: a cold email that takes 4 minutes to write and gets 3-5x the reply rate of a templated blast.

What is your best cold email trick? Drop it below.

#ColdEmail #Sales #B2B #SalesStrategy #FounderSales
```

### Engagement Tips

- Lead with the outcome, not the process. "How to double your reply rate" outperforms "My cold email process."
- Each step must start with an action verb. "Research", "Open", "State" -- not "The first thing you should do is..."
- Include one specific tool, template, or setting per step. Specificity is what makes how-tos actionable.
- Keep steps to 1-2 sentences each. A step that requires a paragraph is actually two steps.
- The result statement is mandatory. Without it, the reader finishes with no clear picture of what they built.
- Invite readers to share their own methods. How-to posts generate excellent comment threads when the audience has alternative approaches.

### Platform Variants

**X/Twitter**: One step per tweet. Tweet 1 = hook with the outcome promise. Tweets 2-N = one step each (action verb + explanation in under 280 chars). Final tweet = result + CTA. Include specific tools/values in each tweet -- they must be actionable in isolation. Use (1/N) thread counters.

**Meta (FB)**: Condense to 3-4 key steps. Facebook audiences prefer quick wins over comprehensive processes. Pair with a carousel or infographic that visualizes the steps. The post text provides the why and the CTA; the visual provides the how. "Save this for later" works well as a CTA on how-to content.

**Meta (IG)**: Carousel format is the natural fit -- one step per slide with visual design. Caption provides the hook, context, and CTA. Each slide: step number + action verb label + one-sentence explanation with visual treatment. Final slide = result + "Save this and try it today." Hashtags in first comment.

**TikTok**: Screen recording or demonstration format. Hook (3s) = "Here is how to [outcome] in [N] steps." Body = show each step with text overlay and voiceover, 5-8 seconds per step. CTA (3s) = "Follow for more [topic] tips." Keep to 3-4 steps maximum for a 30-45 second video. Caption = hook + hashtags.

---

## 5. Personal Lesson

### When to Use

Use the Personal Lesson framework when the post combines vulnerability with insight. This framework differs from Story in that it centers the emotion and the lesson more than the narrative arc. The writer admits a failure, mistake, or misconception and extracts value from it. Choose this framework when the post is less about what happened and more about what the writer learned about themselves.

### Ideal Audience Segments

- **Primary**: Founder-CEO (connects through shared struggles)
- **Secondary**: All segments -- vulnerability with insight is universally engaging

### Recommended Length Mode

Medium. Personal Lesson posts need enough room for the admission and the insight but should not overstay. Long personal lessons risk becoming self-indulgent. Short personal lessons feel incomplete.

### Structure Template

```
Line 1-2: HOOK -- Admit the failure or mistake upfront.
           "I failed at X." or "I was wrong about X for years."
           No hedging. No softening. Direct admission.

Line 3-5: THE MISTAKE -- Describe what was done wrong and why it seemed right at the time.
           This is critical: show that the mistake was rational, not foolish.
           The reader should recognize themselves in the reasoning.

Line 6-8: THE MOMENT -- Describe the specific moment of realization.
           What happened that broke the old mental model?
           A conversation, a result, a data point, a failure.

Line 9-11: THE LESSON -- State the insight clearly.
            Frame it as universal, not personal.
            "The lesson: [insight that applies to the reader]."

Line 12-13: CLOSER -- One sentence on what changed as a result.
             CTA inviting the reader to share their version.

Hashtags: 3-5 relevant tags on the final line.
```

### Example Post

```
I was wrong about delegation for 3 years.

I thought delegating meant assigning tasks and checking results.

So I handed off work, waited for deadlines, and got frustrated when the output did not match my expectations.

The problem was me, not my team.

A mentor asked me one question that changed everything: "Did you delegate the task, or did you delegate the context?"

I had been giving people WHAT to do without explaining WHY it mattered, WHO it was for, and WHAT good looked like.

The lesson: delegation is not about transferring tasks. It is about transferring judgment.

When I started sharing the reasoning behind decisions -- not just the decisions themselves -- my team stopped asking for approval and started making better calls than I would have.

What is the hardest lesson you have learned about leading a team?

#Leadership #Delegation #FounderLessons #Management
```

### Engagement Tips

- The admission must be genuine. Audiences detect false humility. Do not manufacture vulnerability for engagement.
- Show that the mistake was rational. "I thought X because Y" builds empathy. "I was just bad at it" does not.
- The moment of realization needs a specific trigger -- a question someone asked, a metric that dropped, a conversation that shifted thinking. Without the trigger, the lesson feels abstract.
- Frame the lesson outward. "Here is what I learned" is weaker than "Here is what this means for anyone leading a team."
- Personal Lesson posts drive the most saves (bookmarks). Readers return to them. Optimize for depth over virality.
- Avoid ending with generic self-help language. "Believe in yourself" is not a lesson. "Delegate context, not tasks" is.

### Platform Variants

**X/Twitter**: Tweet 1 = the admission ("I was wrong about X for N years."). Tweet 2 = what was done wrong and why it seemed right. Tweet 3 = the moment of realization (include the specific trigger). Tweet 4 = the lesson, stated universally. Tweet 5 = CTA. Personal lessons on X work best when the admission is sharp and the lesson is quotable -- tweet 4 should be retweetable on its own.

**Meta (FB)**: Lean into the emotional dimension. Facebook audiences resonate with vulnerability more than any other platform. Expand the "moment" section -- describe how it felt, not just what happened. Pair with a personal photo if available (candid shots outperform polished images). The CTA should invite readers to share their own lessons in the comments.

**Meta (IG)**: Text-on-image post with the lesson as the visual headline. Caption tells the full story: admission, mistake, moment, lesson. The visual = the one-line lesson in bold typography on a clean background. This format is highly saveable. Long caption (1000-1500 chars) works well here because readers who stop for the visual will read the story.

**TikTok**: Direct-to-camera confessional format. Hook (3s) = "I was wrong about [X] for [N] years. Here is what I learned." Body (20-30s) = tell the mistake and the moment of realization conversationally. Show genuine emotion -- TikTok audiences reward authenticity over polish. Close (3-5s) = state the lesson + "What is your version of this?" Caption = hook + hashtags.

---

## 6. Industry Insight

### When to Use

Use the Industry Insight framework when the post analyzes a trend, data point, or market shift with original perspective. This framework positions the writer as a knowledgeable observer who can interpret signals others miss. Choose it when the topic is grounded in evidence (a report, a market move, a data set) and the writer has a specific interpretation to offer.

### Ideal Audience Segments

- **Primary**: Corporate-CXO (values strategic analysis)
- **Secondary**: Founder-CEO (values trend awareness for decision-making)

### Recommended Length Mode

Medium to Long. Industry Insight posts need room for the evidence and the interpretation. Short format lacks the space for credible analysis.

### Structure Template

```
Line 1-2: HOOK -- Lead with the most surprising data point or trend signal.
           State it as a fact, not an opinion.
           "X just happened. Here is what it means."

Line 3-5: THE DATA -- Present the evidence.
           Reference the source (report, announcement, metric).
           Include 1-2 specific numbers that anchor the analysis.
           Note: place links in the first comment, not in the post body.

Line 6-9: THE INTERPRETATION -- Offer original analysis.
           What does this data mean that most people are missing?
           Connect the data point to a broader trend or implication.
           Name who this affects and how.

Line 10-12: THE IMPLICATION -- Project forward.
             "If this trend continues, X will happen."
             Be specific about timelines and affected parties.

Line 13-15: CLOSER -- State the actionable takeaway.
             What should the reader do differently based on this insight?
             CTA inviting perspectives from others in the industry.

Hashtags: 3-5 relevant tags on the final line.
```

### Example Post

```
SaaS net revenue retention dropped below 100% industry-wide for the first time since 2020.

That one number tells a bigger story.

According to the latest SaaS benchmarks report, median NRR fell to 97% -- meaning the average SaaS company is now shrinking within its existing customer base.

Here is what most people are missing:

This is not a churn problem. Gross retention is actually stable.

It is a contraction problem. Customers are downsizing seats, dropping add-ons, and negotiating lower tiers at renewal.

The companies maintaining 110%+ NRR share one thing: usage-based pricing with natural expansion triggers.

If you are still pricing per-seat with annual contracts, your renewal conversations just got harder.

The implication: the next 18 months will separate companies that grow through product adoption from companies that grow through sales pressure.

Three things to audit this quarter: your expansion triggers, your downgrade friction, and your time-to-value for new features.

What NRR trends are you seeing in your market?

#SaaS #Retention #StartupMetrics #B2B #PricingStrategy
```

### Engagement Tips

- Always cite a source for the data. Unsourced claims undermine the entire framework. Reference the report or announcement and place the link in the first comment.
- The interpretation must add something beyond what the data says on its face. "Revenue is down" is not insight. "Revenue is down because of contraction, not churn, and here is why that matters" is insight.
- Name specific affected parties: "If you are a B2B SaaS founder pricing per-seat" is more powerful than "companies should pay attention."
- Include a forward-looking projection. Industry Insight posts that only describe the present underperform those that predict the near-future.
- Invite perspectives from readers in different segments of the industry. This drives high-quality comment threads from senior operators and executives.
- Avoid presenting opinion as data. Clearly separate what the evidence shows from what the writer believes it means.

### Platform Variants

**X/Twitter**: Tweet 1 = the surprising data point (standalone-readable, shareable). Tweet 2 = source citation and one more number. Tweet 3-4 = the interpretation (what most people are missing). Tweet 5 = the forward-looking implication. Tweet 6 = actionable takeaway + CTA. Industry insight threads on X get saved and shared heavily -- make each tweet quotable. Link to the source in the final tweet.

**Meta (FB)**: Lead with the "so what" rather than the data point. Facebook audiences care about impact, not metrics. "Your SaaS pricing model just became a liability" hooks better than "NRR dropped below 100%." Include a chart or visual of the data if available -- Facebook's algorithm boosts image posts. Keep the analysis conversational, not report-like.

**Meta (IG)**: Data visualization is essential. Create a carousel: slide 1 = the surprising stat in bold typography, slides 2-3 = the data and interpretation as designed graphics, final slide = the actionable takeaway. Caption provides the narrative thread and CTA. Industry insight carousels are among the most-saved content types on Instagram.

**TikTok**: "Did you know" or "This number should scare you" hook format. Show the data point on screen (text overlay or a chart) in the first 3 seconds. Explain the interpretation conversationally (15-25s). Close with the one thing the viewer should do differently (5s). Keep it simple -- one data point, one insight, one action. Caption = hook + hashtags.

---

## 7. Question-Led

### When to Use

Use the Question-Led framework when the post opens with a provocative or thought-provoking question and then explores it. This framework excels at driving comments because it explicitly invites the reader's perspective from line 1. Choose it when the topic is better served by exploration than by declaration -- when the writer wants to think alongside the reader rather than lecture.

### Ideal Audience Segments

- **Primary**: Founder-CEO (engages through shared exploration)
- **Secondary**: Corporate-CXO (values strategic questions)

### Recommended Length Mode

Short to Medium. The question is the engine. A short Question-Led post (just the question + 2-3 lines of context + invitation to answer) can outperform longer formats when the question is strong enough. Medium allows for the writer to explore their own answer before inviting others.

### Structure Template

```
Line 1-2: HOOK -- Ask the question.
           Make it specific, not vague.
           "What would happen if X?" or "Why do we still do X when Y exists?"
           The question must have no obvious right answer.

Line 3-6: EXPLORATION -- Explore the question briefly.
           Offer one perspective or partial answer.
           Introduce a tension or trade-off that makes the question harder.
           Do not resolve it completely -- leave room for the reader.

Line 7-9: THE WRITER'S TAKE -- Share a preliminary position.
           "Here is where I have landed (for now):"
           Signal that the position is open to challenge.

Line 10-11: INVITATION -- Explicitly invite the reader to answer.
             "I genuinely want to know: [restate the question in simpler terms]."
             Make it clear that comments are the point, not the post.

Hashtags: 3-5 relevant tags on the final line.
```

### Example Post

```
What would you build if you could only keep 3 tools?

Not 3 categories. 3 specific tools.

I have been thinking about this all week after my team spent 6 hours debugging a workflow that connected 11 different apps.

11 apps. For a 7-person company.

Here is where I landed:

1. Notion (for everything that needs to be written down)
2. Gmail (for everything external)
3. Stripe (for getting paid)

Everything else -- Slack, Figma, Linear, Loom, Calendly, Zapier -- gone.

Would we be less efficient? Probably.
Would we be less distracted? Definitely.

But I am not sure about my list.

What are your 3? And what is the hardest one to cut?

#Productivity #Startups #ToolStack #SoloFounder #FounderLife
```

### Engagement Tips

- The question must be specific enough to answer but open enough to debate. "What is your favorite book?" is too simple. "What is the one business book that actually changed how you operate?" is specific.
- Explore the question yourself before inviting others. Posts that only ask a question without contributing the writer's own thinking feel lazy.
- Signal genuine curiosity. "I genuinely want to know" or "I have been going back and forth on this" tells readers that comments will be read and valued.
- Respond to comments. Question-Led posts live or die by the comment thread. The writer must participate in the conversation for at least the first 2 hours after posting.
- Avoid yes/no questions. They kill discussion. Every question should require explanation.
- The best Question-Led posts generate comments that are more valuable than the post itself. Design the question to elicit stories, not just opinions.

### Platform Variants

**X/Twitter**: The question alone can be the entire tweet for short mode. One tweet, one question, let the replies flow. For medium mode: tweet 1 = the question, tweet 2 = brief context/exploration, tweet 3 = the writer's take + "What is yours?" Question-Led is the highest-reply format on X. Keep the question under 200 characters so readers can quote-tweet with their answer.

**Meta (FB)**: Facebook is built for discussion. Question-Led posts perform exceptionally well here. Ask the question, provide minimal context (2-3 sentences), and let the comment section do the work. Facebook's algorithm prioritizes posts with high comment counts, so a strong question can snowball. Pair with a simple image (text-on-background with the question) to boost visibility in the feed.

**Meta (IG)**: Use the question as a text-on-image post or Story poll. Caption provides the writer's exploration and take. Stories with poll/quiz stickers are the most natural Instagram format for this framework. For feed posts, the visual = the question in bold typography. CTA: "Drop your answer in the comments" + "Share this to your Story and tag me with your answer."

**TikTok**: Direct-to-camera question format. Hook (3s) = ask the question while looking directly at the camera. Body (10-20s) = share the writer's take briefly. CTA (3s) = "Comment your answer. I am reading every one." TikTok's duet and stitch features make Question-Led content highly shareable -- viewers can respond with their own video. Caption = the question (abbreviated to fit 150 chars) + hashtags.

---

## Framework Mixing

When generating multiple variations of the same topic (via `/founder-os:ideate:variations`), select different frameworks for each variation to provide the user with distinct options. A single topic can be expressed through any framework:

| Topic: "We hit $1M ARR" | Framework | Angle |
|--------------------------|-----------|-------|
| Story | The moment we hit the number and what nearly derailed us |
| Listicle | "7 things that actually moved the needle to $1M" |
| Contrarian Take | "Hitting $1M ARR taught me that ARR is the wrong metric" |
| Personal Lesson | "I almost missed $1M because I was optimizing the wrong thing" |
| Industry Insight | "What $1M ARR looks like in 2026 vs 2020 -- the bar has moved" |
| Question-Led | "At what point did your startup start feeling real?" |
| How-To | "How we went from $0 to $1M ARR in 14 months: the exact playbook" |

Never generate two variations using the same framework unless the user explicitly requests it.

When generating cross-platform variations of the same topic, adapt the framework to each platform's strengths. A Story on LinkedIn becomes a thread on X, a carousel on Instagram, and a 30-second direct-to-camera video script on TikTok -- same framework, different execution.
